stage.name: What is CVA6: do not start with random RTL
stage.description: A source-grounded OpenHW SlideKit microcourse that establishes the official engineering positioning of CVA6 before reading RTL.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: What is CVA6: do not start with random RTL

content.title: What is CVA6: do not start with random RTL
content.subtitle: Lesson 1 answers one question: what is the official engineering identity of CVA6?
content.thesis: When reading an open-source processor project, the most expensive mistake is opening random RTL files before defining the object. Define CVA6 first, then the source tree becomes much less noisy.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 overview and configurable family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is a RISC-V compatible application processor core configurable as CV32A6 or CV64A6, with optional MMU, PMP, FPU, cache organization and size, and many parameters.

content.speakerNotes: Let us start with a simple question: what exactly is CVA6? Many learners open an open-source processor repository and immediately jump into the RTL directory. Ten minutes later, the module names feel like a maze. A better first move is to define the object. The official material points to a RISC-V compatible application processor core, with six-stage, single-issue, in-order positioning, and a configurable family around it.

actions.text: Let us start with a simple question: what exactly is CVA6? Many learners open an open-source processor repository and immediately jump into the RTL directory. Ten minutes later, the module names feel like a maze. A better first move is to define the object. The official material points to a RISC-V compatible application processor core, with six-stage, single-issue, in-order positioning, and a configurable family around it.

scenes.title: Start with three source types, not random files

content.title: Start with three source types, not random files
content.subtitle: This lesson builds a minimal evidence map: README for positioning, User Manual for user-facing scope, and Design Docs for configuration-specific internals.

cards.label: README
cards.title: One-line project identity
cards.body: It tells you CVA6 is not an arbitrary RTL pile, but a RISC-V core with explicit microarchitecture labels.

cards.label: User Manual
cards.title: Who uses this IP
cards.body: Software, hardware integration, coprocessor, implementation, and verification engineers read the manual with different questions.

cards.label: Design Docs
cards.title: Internals depend on configuration
cards.body: A design document is often tied to a concrete variant. Do not generalize one variant into all CVA6 builds.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: User Manual target audience
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual targets software programmers, hardware integrators, coprocessor architects, implementation engineers, and verification engineers.

sourceAnchors.title: Configuration-specific design documentation
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Configuration-specific design documentation describes internal architecture and helps avoid mixing general CVA6 statements with a specific variant.

content.speakerNotes: This is the reading order. The README is good for high-level positioning: it gives labels such as six-stage, single-issue, and in-order. The User Manual is closer to a user-facing guide: different engineering roles can use the same IP but care about different questions. Design documents go deeper, but usually through a specific configuration. That is why random file browsing is a weak first strategy.

actions.text: This is the reading order. The README is good for high-level positioning: it gives labels such as six-stage, single-issue, and in-order. The User Manual is closer to a user-facing guide: different engineering roles can use the same IP but care about different questions. Design documents go deeper, but usually through a specific configuration. That is why random file browsing is a weak first strategy.

scenes.title: One sentence: a configurable RISC-V application processor core

content.title: One sentence: a configurable RISC-V application processor core
content.subtitle: Once the positioning is clear, you can separate architecture questions from integration questions.

columns.label: Core identity
columns.title: It is first a processor core
bullets: six-stage pipeline is a high-level microarchitecture clue
single-issue and in-order guide how we later read Issue and Commit
TLB, PTW, and branch prediction tell us this is not a toy CPU

columns.label: Configurable family
columns.title: It is not one fixed instance
bullets: CV32A6 and CV64A6 point to different family directions
MMU, PMP, FPU, and cache behavior may depend on configuration
many parameters mean every claim needs a variant context
choose the configuration first, then draw conclusions

anchor.label: Engineering sentence
anchor.text: CVA6 is a configurable RISC-V application processor core, not a complete SoC.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 overview and configurable family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is a RISC-V compatible application processor core configurable as CV32A6 or CV64A6, with optional MMU, PMP, FPU, cache organization and size, and many parameters.

content.speakerNotes: I would compress the entry definition into one engineering sentence: CVA6 is a configurable RISC-V application processor core, not a complete SoC. Every word matters. Application processor core means it is meant for a richer processor class than a tiny teaching CPU. Configurable means you must ask which variant you are reading. Core means system-level peripherals and board flows are not automatically inside the CVA6 boundary.

actions.text: I would compress the entry definition into one engineering sentence: CVA6 is a configurable RISC-V application processor core, not a complete SoC. Every word matters. Application processor core means it is meant for a richer processor class than a tiny teaching CPU. Configurable means you must ask which variant you are reading. Core means system-level peripherals and board flows are not automatically inside the CVA6 boundary.

scenes.title: Do not read CVA6 as a complete SoC

content.title: Do not read CVA6 as a complete SoC
content.subtitle: The User Manual scope material separates what is inside the IP from what sits around it.

items.title: Core view
items.body: Pipeline, CSR, optional MMU/PMP/FPU, and performance counters are core topics we will keep unpacking.

items.title: Interface view
items.body: AXI, P-Mesh, and CV-X-IF let the core integrate with a system, but they are not the whole SoC.

items.title: Outside the core
items.body: CLINT, PLIC, debug module, and board-level boot flows should not be casually explained as CVA6 internals.

items.title: Configuration view
items.body: MMU, PMP, FPU, and cache organization may change with configuration, so the variant always matters.

content.takeaway: Lesson 1 takeaway: CVA6 has a clear IP boundary. If the boundary is blurry, RTL reading gets messy fast.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

content.speakerNotes: The second key idea is boundary. CVA6 is not a board-level system, and it is not an all-inclusive SoC from boot ROM to every peripheral. The User Manual scope tells us what belongs inside the IP and what belongs outside. This matters in practice. If a question is about AXI integration, exception routing, or a debug block, you first need to ask whether you are looking at CVA6 internals, an interface, or the surrounding system.

actions.text: The second key idea is boundary. CVA6 is not a board-level system, and it is not an all-inclusive SoC from boot ROM to every peripheral. The User Manual scope tells us what belongs inside the IP and what belongs outside. This matters in practice. scenes.title: Beginner reading flow: identity, boundary, configuration, microarchitecture

content.title: Beginner reading flow: identity, boundary, configuration, microarchitecture
content.subtitle: The series moves in small steps instead of stuffing every module into one lesson.

steps.title: Boundary
steps.body: Use the scope diagram to separate inside from outside.

steps.title: Configuration
steps.body: Confirm which variant you are reading.

steps.title: Microarchitecture
steps.body: Then enter pipeline, frontend, issue, and memory.

content.takeaway: A stable reading order saves more time than opening a dozen RTL files and hoping the structure reveals itself.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

sourceAnchors.title: Configuration-specific design documentation
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Configuration-specific design documentation describes internal architecture and helps avoid mixing general CVA6 statements with a specific variant.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the reading flow for the next lessons. Step one is this lesson: identity. Step two is boundary: IP, core, cache, interface, and SoC are not interchangeable words. Step three is configuration, because CVA6 is a configurable family and conclusions can change by variant. Step four is microarchitecture, where we finally walk through pipeline, frontend, issue, memory, and commit. The order is deliberate.

Step four is microarchitecture, where we finally walk through pipeline, frontend, issue, memory, and commit. The order is deliberate.

scenes.title: Checkpoint: the official positioning of CVA6

questions.question: Which sentence is the best entry-level engineering positioning for CVA6?

options.label: A configurable RISC-V application processor core
A complete SoC with every peripheral and debug block included
A fixed single-cycle teaching CPU with no configuration
A generic SystemVerilog template library unrelated to RISC-V

questions.analysis: The README and User Manual position CVA6 as a RISC-V compatible application processor core and emphasize the configurable family.

questions.question: Why should you care about configuration before reading CVA6 code?

options.label: CVA6 has different family directions such as CV32A6 and CV64A6
MMU, PMP, FPU, and cache-related capabilities may depend on configuration
Every configuration has exactly the same internal behavior
Design documents often explain internals through a concrete configuration

questions.analysis: Configuration affects family direction, optional units, and the scope of a design document. Option C contradicts the configurable-family model.

questions.question: On your first day reading CVA6, what is the safest starting point?

options.label: Open a random RTL file first and figure out the object later
Read README and User Manual sections on positioning, scope, and configuration first
Assume CVA6 already includes a full SoC and search for peripherals
Ignore official documents and infer the architecture from file names only

questions.analysis: The series uses source-grounded reading: begin with official positioning and boundaries before drilling into implementation files.

actions.text: Let us do a short checkpoint. This is not a vocabulary quiz; it checks three engineering judgments. First, can you position CVA6 as a configurable RISC-V application processor core rather than a full SoC? Second, do you know why configuration matters before reading code? Third, can you choose a stable first reading path instead of following random file names?

scenes.title: Summary: define the object before reading source

content.title: Define the object before reading source
takeaways: CVA6 is best introduced as a configurable RISC-V application processor core.
Do not merge core IP, interfaces, system peripherals, and a full SoC into one mental bucket.
Configuration awareness matters: ask which variant you are reading.
README, User Manual, and Design Docs have different jobs in the reading workflow.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: what CVA6 is not - IP, core, cache, and SoC boundaries.
content.closing: If you remember only one sentence from this lesson: do not rush into RTL; define CVA6 first.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 README architecture claim
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is positioned as a configurable 6-stage, single-issue, in-order RISC-V CPU with separate TLBs, hardware PTW, and branch prediction.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 overview and configurable family
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CVA6 is a RISC-V compatible application processor core configurable as CV32A6 or CV64A6, with optional MMU, PMP, FPU, cache organization and size, and many parameters.

sourceAnchors.title: Scope of the CVA6 IP
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The documented IP includes the CVA6 core, L1 write-through cache, optional FPU/MMU/PMP, CSR, performance counters, AXI interface, P-Mesh interface, and CV-X-IF, while several system-level blocks are outside the IP.

content.speakerNotes: Let us wrap up. The entry definition of CVA6 is not empty wording; it controls every later reading decision. CVA6 is a configurable RISC-V application processor core. It is not a complete SoC, and it is not a repository without boundaries. In the next lesson, we will keep that same discipline and separate IP, core, cache, interface, and SoC responsibilities before reading more implementation detail.
